title: [2019] CCJ 05 (OJ) — Trinidad Cement Limited,  Arawak Cement Limited, Rock Hard Distribution Ltd and Rock Hard Cement Ltd v State of Trinidad and Tobago, The State of Barbados, The Caribbean Community
text:
[2019] CCJ 5 (OJ) 
 
 
 
IN THE CARIBBEAN COURT OF JUSTICE 
Original Jurisdiction 
  
CCJ Application No TTOJ2018/001 
Between 
                                                   Trinidad Cement Limited                                    Claimant 
And 
                                   The State of Trinidad and Tobago                       Defendant 
And 
                                               Rock Hard Distribution Limited 
             First Intervener 
And 
                            Mootilal Ramhit and Sons Contracting Limited       Second Intervener 
 
And 
CCJ Application No TTOJ2018/002 
 
   Between 
                                              Trinidad Cement Limited &                                Claimants 
 
 
     Arawak Cement Company Limited 
And 
                                       The State of Barbados  
 
              Defendant 
And 
                                              Rock Hard Cement Limited   
              Intervener 
 
And  
 
CCJ Application No SLUOJ2018/001 
 
  Between 
                                  Rock Hard Distribution Limited                              Claimant 
And 
                          The State of Trinidad and Tobago  
      First Defendant 
And 
                                         The Caribbean Community  
 
  Second Defendant

[2019] CCJ 5 (OJ) 
 
 
And 
CCJ Application No BBOJ2018/001 
Between 
                                    Rock Hard Cement Limited 
                       Claimant 
And 
                                        The State of Barbados  
 
         First Defendant 
And 
                                              The Caribbean Community   
    Second Defendant 
 
 (Consolidated by order of this Court dated the 12th day of February 2019) 
 
THE COURT, 
 
composed of A Saunders, President and J Wit, W Anderson, M Rajnauth-Lee and D Barrow, 
Judges 
 
Having regard to the Orders made by the Court on the 6th day of August 2019  
 
And after considering the written and oral submissions made on behalf of: 
 
- 
Trinidad Cement Ltd and Arawak Cement Company Ltd, by Mr Reginald 
Armour, SC, Mr Gilbert Peterson SC, Mr Gregory Pantin, Mr Miguel Vasquez and Mr 
Raphael Ajodhia, Attorneys-at-Law 
 
- 
Rock Hard Cement Ltd and Rock Hard Distribution Ltd, by Mr Allan Wood QC 
and Ms Symone Mayhew, Attorneys-at-Law 
 
- 
The State of Barbados, by Ms Donna Brathwaite QC and Ms Gayl Scott, Attorneys-
at-Law 
 
- 
Mootilal Ramhit and Sons Contracting Limited, by Mr Dinesh Rambally, Mr Kiel 
Taklalsingh and Mr Stefan Ramkissoon, Attorneys-at-Law 
 
- 
The Caribbean Community, by Dr Corlita Babb-Schaefer and Mr O’Neil Francis, 
Attorneys-at-Law 
 
- 
The State of Trinidad and Tobago, by Ms. Deborah Peake, SC, Ms. Tamara Toolsie, 
Mr. Brent James and Ms. Radha Sookdeo, Attorneys-at-Law 
 
 
Delivers on the 28th day of November 2019, the following: 
 
 
SUPPLEMENTARY JUDGMENT AS TO COSTS 
 
[1] 
The Court must now decide the entitlement of the Parties to costs, arising from its 
Judgment dated 6th August 2019 awarding “costs to the successful parties in this

[2019] CCJ 5 (OJ) 
 
 
matter.”1 As directed, the Parties made submissions as to the quantum or percentages 
of costs to which they were entitled or liable, and were subsequently heard. 
[2] 
It will be helpful to repeat the introduction in the Judgment:  
These proceedings mark the culmination of multifaceted litigation that began 
with the filing of an Application for Special Leave on 24 April 2018. That 
Application concerned the classification and tariff payable in respect of 
certain cement (‘Rock Hard Cement’) imported from outside the Caribbean 
Community (sometimes referred to in this judgment as ‘CARICOM’). The 
Application spawned numerous other applications and claims which led to 
the delivery of five separate judgments and the prescription of myriad orders 
by this Court. The issue that remains for determination is the very narrow one 
with which this odyssey began, namely: whether Rock Hard Cement 
imported from Turkey and Portugal is to be classified as ‘Building cement 
(grey)’, thereby attracting a tariff of 15%, or as ‘Other hydraulic cement’, on 
which a tariff of 0-5% is payable.2 
[3] 
The overarching determination of the Court was that the Council for Trade and 
Economic Development (‘COTED’) had validly classified Rock Hard cement as 
‘Other hydraulic cement’. The Court therefore disposed of the four cases as follows:  
-- The Originating Application No. TTOJ2018/001 Trinidad Cement Limited 
v The State of Trinidad and Tobago; Rock Hard Distribution Limited and 
Mootilal Ramhit and Sons Contracting Limited, Intervening (‘OA No. 1’) is 
dismissed; 
-- The Originating Application No. TTOJ2018/002 Trinidad Cement Limited 
and Arawak Cement Company Limited v The State of Barbados, Rock Hard 
Cement Limited, Intervening (‘OA No. 2’) is dismissed; 
-- The Originating Application No. BBOJ2018/001 Rock Hard Cement 
Limited v The State of Barbados and The Caribbean Community (‘OA No. 
3’) is granted to the extent indicated in this Judgment; and 
-- The Originating Application No. SLU2018/001 Rock Hard Distribution 
Limited v The State of Trinidad and Tobago and The Caribbean Community 
(‘OA No. 4’) is granted to the extent indicated in this Judgment; 
[4] 
Rule 31.1(2) of the Original Jurisdiction Rules 2019 recognises the general rule that 
costs follow the event and empowers the Court to order the unsuccessful party to 
pay costs for the successful party and where there are several unsuccessful parties, 
 
1 Trinidad Cement Limited v The State of Trinidad and Tobago and Rock Hard Distribution Limited and Mootilal Ramhit and Sons 
Contracting Limited Intervening; Trinidad Cement Limited & Arawak Cement Company Limited v The State of Barbados and Rock Hard 
Cement Limited Intervening; Rock Hard Distribution Limited v The State of Trinidad and Tobago and the Caribbean Community and Rock 
Hard Cement Limited v The State of Barbados and The Caribbean Community [2019] CCJ 4 (OJ), [96]. 
2 Ibid, [1].

[2019] CCJ 5 (OJ) 
 
 
to decide how the costs are to be shared among them3. In arriving at its present 
decision on costs, the Court has fully considered the course the proceedings took, 
including when and how the four cases commenced, who were Parties in which 
cases, the various issues that arose, how they evolved, the interim decisions and 
ultimately, the consolidation. The Court has decided that the fair approach for 
determining the entitlements and liabilities of the Parties is to do so on the basis that 
these were consolidated proceedings, while paying due regard to the course of the 
proceedings. 
[5] 
On that approach, Rock Hard Distribution Ltd (‘RHDL’) and Rock Hard Cement 
Ltd (‘RHCL’) are the overall successful Parties. Each of these two entities, who are 
related parties, appeared in two of the four proceedings4 and were represented in all 
proceedings by the same Attorneys-at-law, Mr. Allan Wood QC and Ms Symone 
Mayhew. The Rock Hard parties were not successful on all issues and the Court 
must take account of this. 
 
[6] 
Trinidad Cement Ltd (‘TCL’) and Arawak Cement Company Ltd (‘ACCL’) are 
related parties. TCL appeared in only two of the original four cases5 but was the 
leading protagonist, as was demonstrated after the consolidation. ACCL appeared in 
only the case against Barbados, as Claimants along with TCL.6 The two companies 
were separately represented, each by Senior Counsel and a number of Junior 
Counsel but agreed that an order for costs made against them jointly would be 
reasonable. While the two companies were losing Parties, they did not lose on all 
issues. 
 
[7] 
The State of Trinidad and Tobago was the Defendant in the first-commenced case, 
brought by TCL and was also Defendant in the claim brought by RHDL. The State 
was in essence a losing party but did not lose on all issues. 
 
[8] 
The State of Barbados was the Defendant in the two claims brought respectively by 
TCL and ACCL7, and by RHCL.8  It won on the central issue of classification, but 
 
3 Rule 31.1(2) of the Original Jurisdiction Rules, 2019 provides that: 
(a) 
The unsuccessful party shall be ordered to pay the costs if they have been applied for in the successful party’s pleadings. 
(b) Where there are several unsuccessful parties, the Court shall decide how the costs are to be shared among them. 
4 RHDL as the Intervener in TTOJ2018001 and the Claimant in SLU2018/001 and RHCL as the Intervener in TTOJ2018/002 and the 
Claimant in BBOJ2018/001. 
5 TTOJ2018/001 and TTOJ2018/002. 
6 TTOJ2018/002. 
7 TTOJ2018/002. 
8 BBOJ2018/001.

[2019] CCJ 5 (OJ) 
 
 
the unjustified stance the State took on the derogation issue unnecessarily increased 
costs. 
[9] 
Mootilal Ramhit and Sons Contracting Ltd were Interveners in the first-commenced 
claim by TCL and had a common interest with RHDL as to classification. Their 
principal arguments at the beginning of the proceedings were that a domestic court 
and not this Court, or the Council for Trade and Economic development (COTED), 
should exercise jurisdiction to determine the rate of duty to be imposed on Rock 
Hard’s cement. After the consolidation, Ramhit were content to file a notice stating 
they adopted the submissions filed by Rock Hard.9 Although Ramhit had Senior 
Counsel and the largest number of junior attorneys in attendance, it was a limited 
contribution they were required to make on the substantive issues. The reality is that 
it was RHDL and not Ramhit who principally fought the case against TCL and bore 
the burden of making the major arguments in this regard. For these reasons Ramhit 
should be entitled, on the taxation of costs, to forty percent (40%) of costs for a 
senior and one junior counsel.    
 
[10] 
That determination of issues and proportion of recovery leads to an overall 
consideration of the substantive issues in the proceedings and success on them. The 
four substantive issues were broadly: classification, derogation, jurisdiction and 
interim measures. 
 
[11] 
Classification was the major issue and the two Rock Hard entities were successful on 
it, as they were also on the jurisdiction of the Court to decide the dispute as to 
classification. They were also successful on the issue of whether permission was 
needed from CARICOM for the State to abandon a derogation that has been granted 
to it. However, they lost on the competence of COTED to make a non-judicial 
determination on classification. Their loss on the issue whether the Court should have 
granted interim measures and should have discharged them does not count against 
them because, in the end, TCL was not entitled to the alleged right that the interim 
measures were granted to protect. 
 
[12] 
Having considered the matter of costs in the round, the Court orders that the two 
Rock Hard entities shall recover eighty percent of their costs, fit for senior and one 
junior Counsel. For certainty, the Court clarifies that the Rock Hard entities are 
 
9 TTOJ2018/001 dated 18 April 2019 p 8023.

[2019] CCJ 5 (OJ) 
 
 
entitled to one award of costs in the consolidated proceedings and not individual or 
separate awards in each of the four cases. The taxing officer will ensure there is no 
double recovery.  Sixty percent of those costs shall be borne by TCL and ACCL, 
twenty percent shall be borne by the State of Trinidad and Tobago, and twenty 
percent shall be borne by the State of Barbados. 
 
[13] 
As stated above, the State of Barbados successfully defended the claims brought by 
TCL and ACCL but unnecessarily protracted the litigation by failing: 
(i) 
to accept that the derogation they obtained from CARICOM included 
‘other hydraulic cement’ and; 
(ii) 
 to revoke the statutory instruments10 that gave effect to that 
derogation following their decision in 2015 to reimpose the CET of 
5% on ‘other hydraulic cement’.  
 
In those circumstances, the Court therefore orders that the State of Barbados shall 
recover from TCL and ACCL forty percent (40%) of its costs of defending the 
claims, fit for a senior and a junior Counsel. 
 
[14] 
Costs for Ramhit have already been awarded at the rate of forty percent (40%) and 
these costs are to be borne by TCL. 
 
[15] 
A final observation concerns the submission made by TCL at the post-judgment 
hearing on costs. TCL argued that the litigation initiated by them had led to the 
pronouncement of judgments on a number of matters of great importance to 
CARICOM, resulting in the development of the jurisprudence, the clarification of 
doubts and uncertainties and guidance for future dealings. They, therefore, argued 
that it was unfair that they should bear the costs burden for the attainment of that 
result. The argument has substance. However, the Rock Hard entities were forced to 
resist TCL’s claims as a matter of necessity and not choice. They cannot, therefore, 
be required to bear the costs of participating in litigating issues that they did not 
initiate. It is because of this fundamental principle that the Court cannot revisit the 
costs order it pronounced in its judgment, even if that course were otherwise 
available. 
 
 
10 Customs Tariff (Amendment) (No. 5) Order, Gazetted 31 December 2004 and Customs Tariff (Amendment) (No. 9) Order, Gazetted 31 
December 2009.

[2019] CCJ 5 (OJ) 
 
 
Orders   
[16] 
In summary, the Court makes the following Orders for costs: 
(1) 
Rock Hard Cement Limited and Rock Hard Distribution Limited shall jointly 
recover 80% of their costs incurred in the proceedings to be paid: 
i. 
60% by Trinidad Cement Limited and Arawak Cement Company 
Limited. 
ii. 
20% by The State of Trinidad and Tobago; 
iii. 
20% by the State of Barbados.  
 
(2) 
Trinidad Cement Limited to pay 40% of the costs incurred by the Second 
Intervener Mootilal Ramhit and Sons Contracting Limited in the proceedings. 
 
(3) 
Trinidad Cement Limited and Arawak Cement Company Limited to pay 40% of 
the costs incurred by the State of Barbados in the proceedings and 
 
(4) 
The Caribbean Community shall bear its own costs.11  
 
 
/s/ A Saunders 
______________________________________ 
The Hon Mr Justice A Saunders (President) 
 
                  
 
/s/ J Wit 
 
 
 
   /s/ W Anderson 
 
     ________________________        
 __________________________ 
      The Hon Mr Justice J Wit  
 The Hon Justice W Anderson 
 
 
 
                    /s/ M Rajnauth-Lee 
 
    
         /s/ D Barrow 
  __________________________________ 
    ____________________________ 
The Hon Mme Justice M Rajnauth-Lee 
     The Hon Mr Justice D Barrow 
 
 
 
 
11 Appropriately, they did not ask for costs and no Party sought costs from them.
parties: Trinidad Cement Limited,  Arawak Cement Limited, Rock Hard Distribution Ltd and Rock Hard Cement Ltd v State of Trinidad and Tobago, The State of Barbados, The Caribbean Community
